speaker: interviewer
text: Date is the 31st of August, 1996. My name is Anne Lunsky. I'm interviewing Lily Lipman in Johannesburg, South Africa, in English. And it's tape four. Is there anything you'd like to add before we have a look at the photographs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there are a couple of stories that I think are relevant for the times. Not necessarily with my work in the displaced persons camps, but the-- my impressions of people. I think the first one that really had a most traumatic effect was an experience I had with Gertrude Starnever, who was the girl who was born in Prague, but family moved to Vienna. And she came to work from Britain as a British social worker, psychologist. Gertrude and I were very friendly. We often went out and saw the country together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this when you were in Rosenheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were in-- yes. This was already in Rosenheim. My-- my experiences beyond Rosenheim were in Mittenwald and Bad Reichenhall. But I can mention that later. This was in Rosenheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one morning, Gertrude came to me and said, Lily, I would like to go and visit the place where I lived in Vienna. Will you come with me? I don't have the courage to go on my own. And of course, my attitude was if I can see the world, then it's fun. I hadn't been to Vienna, and this was a marvelous experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I felt that there was something about Gertrude that was so intense, that she had to go. And so we had-- there was a long weekend. In Germany, incidentally, when we worked there, we had every American holiday, every Jewish holiday, and every German holiday. So we had quite a lot of-- of holidays. And this was a long weekend that we were going. We caught the-- we-- we were in Rosenheim, so we got a lift to-- to Salzburg. From Salzburg, we got a train to Vienna. And we got to Vienna, and I could see that Gertrude was a little shaky and rather sort of excited and-- and worried about what was going to happen. Well, we got-- we got a bus or a-- I can't remember was a bus or a tram, but we got to the block of apartments where her family was-- had lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by then I could see that she was very agitated. And she says, I've got to speak to the-- to the-- the-- I would call him the caretaker. And she looked around and she eventually found a man. And she said, you're the caretaker, aren't you? This is all in German, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he looked at her. And he looked at her. And she says, my name is Gertrude Starnever. And he looked at her and in an absolute horror, he says, Gertrude. Fraulein Gertrude, [NON-ENGLISH]. And she answered him in German, yes. I'm very much alive. I want to see what's happened to my family. So he said, well, your family were taken away, and the people next door went and took the furniture and put it into their homes, into their apartments. And, of course, by that time, I mean, I'm even shaky now when I think about it. Gertrude said, Lily, please, you must come with me. I've got to-- I've got to see what's happened. So we went. I think it was two floors we went up. And we knocked on the door next to the-- she said, well, that was our flat. We call them flats. British call them flats. Americans call them apartments. She said, that was my family's flat. But I'm not even going to go in there, because she'd learned from the caretaker that the people next door were the ones who had taken the furniture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she knocked on the door and a woman answered. And she said in German, what do you want? So she said, I'm Gertrude Starnever, and my parents lived next door. And I understand that you have my parents' furniture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this woman started stuttering and saying, oh, yes, yes, yes. You're Fraulein Starnever. Please come inside. I want to show you. Your parents were taken away, and we told them we would look after the furniture for them until they got back. And by that time, of course, Gertrude was in a state of absolute hysteria. And she looked at this woman. And she spat at her, and she said, you stole my parents' furniture. You didn't have any intention of looking after it. She spat at her, and she says, Lily, let's get out of here. And she ran down the steps and away. And, of course, I followed her, shaking with her. And she went out of the apartment house, and she sat on the pavement and she vomited. And she cried. And she vomited. And she said, Lily, let's get out of here. I can't. I can't stand it. I can't believe that people would lie. Let's get out. And we came back. She was very disturbed for quite a long time. And I was disturbed by it as well, because she had relived something that she had really forgotten. She realized then, that of course, that her parents were-- had been killed in the concentration camp. She never quite knew where. And she came back. We came back to Rosenheim. She never spoke about that incident again, but she was obviously very, very, very, very perturbed and very distressed by what she had experienced. And I was too. I think it made me realize that there was tremendous trauma in her, and in her experiences, even though she was in England right through the war. That she was old enough to realize what had been going on. And we kept in touch. She then left. I think then she left for England, and I-- when I left for South Africa, we were in touch. And about three years after that, I got a letter from her in London to say that she was going to be reunited with her boyfriend and she was going to Chicago. And this-- that in itself is a story of its own. She had met this young man. I'm not sure whether in Prague or in Vienna. I think it was in Prague. And he was studying to be an engineer. And then the family moved to Vienna. And then she went to England. And she'd forgotten about him. And he had, in the meantime, somehow managed to get to America. And he graduated as an engineer. I think maybe in Europe. But anyway he was an engineer in Chicago, and he was determined to find her. And he went to Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he went to all sorts of agencies, Jewish agencies, and Red Cross, to find if there was a-- a Gertrude Starnever on any books. And he eventually found her in London, and he wrote to her and said, please, will you come? I want to marry you. And the letter I got from her was to say that she-- he had found her, and she was going to Chicago to get married. And they got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were still in touch. And then one year when I went to the states, she wrote to say that I've got to go to Chicago, which I did. And we were re-united. And it was a most exciting, but sad, experience. Sad because her husband was now in hospital and very, very ill. But they had been together for a good many years, and had the most wonderful relationship. They-- I think it was about 10 years, perhaps even longer, that they were married in Chicago. And she said, please, come and meet him. I can't remember his name now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, you must meet him. He knows so much. He knows more about you almost than I do, because all the pictures that I had of you. And we went to visit him. And a lovely charming man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was crying. I was crying. She was crying. And then when we'd seen him, we went back to her apartment and we spoke right through the night. We looked through all the pictures that she had of us in Rosenheim, and then the pictures of her marriage and the times they had together. And I don't think we slept that night. And the next day I had to go. I went back, and we kept in touch. And she used to send me Christmas gifts. But I think he died about a year later. And she was really sad, but she had a-- quite a large psychology practice in Chicago, and she carried on with that. And then somehow I lost touch. So I don't really know what's happened to her now, but it was a very, very important part of my-- my experience in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the protests in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were very interesting. In '47, the Haganah and the-- the Jewish underground was very active. And of course, the Jewish agency people in our camps were in touch with the people in Palestine. And they were-- I think it was just after the King David-- there was a-- a-- a King David bombing. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the King-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] the King David bombing-- I'm sorry. Can you-- you can't switch off? You were saying about the protests?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is still on? This was I think early '47, and there were a lot of-- a lot of incidents in Palestine, the underground against the British. They felt the British must get out. There was the King David episode, the bombing of the King David Hotel, and lots of protests in Palestine and in other parts. And the Jewish agency felt that they needed a protest in Rosenheim. So they organized that we would have a protest. Of course, I wasn't told about it, because I wouldn't have agreed to it, and Miss Richman wouldn't have agreed to it. They organized the-- the children in the Madrichim to make posters about that the British must leave, that-- I think the-- the protest said we want to be free as you are free in your country. Please help us. And they had banners made. And they walked round and round and round the camps. And I think it might have even coincided with the period that the American senators came to the camp. But we had-- we had some journalists or-- or visitors, because that was perhaps behind the protests, so that the children would-- would walk up and down the-- the camps with these-- with these posters, and the journalists and the congressmen would see that they must take the side of the Jews and the underground, and not of the British. It went off really successfully, as everything that [NON-ENGLISH] and the Jewish agency arranged, organized. And I was just an observer, neither-- more against than for, because at that stage I was still a-- a South African British citizen, so my sympathies were not really with-- with Palestine, with the-- with the Jewish agency. But it was a very successful march. And I think Miss Richman has always accepted what [NON-ENGLISH] was doing. And I think that Andy was also rather sympathetic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you explain the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a painting done of me by a German painter. The villa we lived in, Rosenheim, was about a 20 minute walk to the camp. And I usually walked with the rest of the UNRRA crowd, but one morning I was either late or early. I had to be at the camp. So I walked on my own, and as I was walking to the camp, a young German chap, he ran after me and said Fraulein, Fraulein, please come here. I want to paint a picture of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, whatever for? He said, I have watched you walking to where you are going to every day, and you're always with people. And today you're on your own. Please, will you come and have a few sittings at my house? But you must be in uniform. So I-- I think I was more flattered than frightened, and I said yes, I would come. And I think I had about two two-hour sittings. And this is what he produced. I was in uniform, and on the side was my AJDC badge.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about I think two weeks later, he found me again. Actually, I was with people. And when he came running out of his house, I came up to him, and asked him-- he spoke very poor English. And I spoke very poor German.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he indicated that the picture was finished. And would I come and collect it when I had some time? Which I did that afternoon, and he presented me with this. And I said, must I pay you? He said, no, no. I want you to have it, because I think you are such a lovely lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think weeks later I brought him some cigarettes and some dollars. And that seemed to make me feel as if I paid him for the picture. And I think it-- it-- it was the way I looked. It was a very good representation of the way I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1946. That was the-- at the beginning of my tour. I think it was about October, or November, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin writing

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in about '79. And I finished it over about 18 months, and then I had to get someone to type it and to bind it. So I think it was completed, as it stands now, in 1981.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the main contents?

speaker: interviewee
text: The contents were really my experiences in Germany from the day I left South Africa until I came back to South Africa. It was from June '46 to February '47. Oh, no, that's only-- no, no. Sorry, it's from June '46 to February '48. I was in Germany for 18 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the picture about?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a group picture of some children at Rosenheim with their Madrichim. The children and the Madrichim. It was the first group picture that was taken by one of the photographers from the Madrichim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any people that you could remember names of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I can't remember. I think that it-- it was rather early on in my stay. And if you can see in the background, they were divided into blocks. And I think this was block A and B. In the background, you can see the sign, block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't remember their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: --people?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were just residents at the Rosenheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '46. December '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the first nurses school that we opened in Rosenheim. We got the equipment from the-- from UNRRA and American army. I think the American army bought it for us. These are the nursery school teachers with two of the pupils in the nursery school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take those photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. Perhaps it was me. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the school room. The first school room, with a-- rather few-- few books and things. But this was the first school room that was established in Rosenheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of those children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. These children are now in their 50s, maybe even 60.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where they came from, which camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were not children from the camps. These were children who the Jewish agency had brought from Poland and who were separated from their parents. But they had had no schooling in Siberia. This was their first introduction to education. This was the first workshop, the ORT workshop, that was established at Rosenheim. I'm not quite sure what this was, because they're-- if they were women, then it was sewing. Because the women did the sewing and the men did the leather work. Now, this was the leather, the making of shoes and the repairing of shoes. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] They were artisans and craftsman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they also were not from the camps, but from-- they had lived in Siberia during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the gymnasium, which was donated to us by the Americans. And they actually put it up for us in one of the large halls that we had. That's me on the right hand side, and next to me is [NON-ENGLISH] from the Jewish-- the Jewish group. And, oh, just above me is Misha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish-- [NON-ENGLISH] was from the Jewish agency.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Misha, again, was from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Misha was one of the DPs who married Eva, who I mentioned early on. And he was also a great one for the-- he was a [NON-ENGLISH], who was a great one with the gymnasium. He did gym. He worked him-- in the gym every-- every day. And these are the two Americans who-- who befriended-- who I befriended and asked for a donation of the gymnasium and all the equipment. And they arranged for the equipment to be brought and erected for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm afraid not. But they were very, very generous and very enthusiastic. And they even came and-- and taught the children how to use the equipment. This is a very interesting picture. It was the first arts and crafts exhibition at Rosenheim, and the Rosenheim children's camp, organized by, as one sees there, Jewish agency, AJDC, ORT, and UNRRA. And the children were asked to draw and make any item that they thought would be interesting for adults to look at. And it took place at the Rosenheim center. And many, many visitors came from other camps to have a look at it. I think ours was the first one in Germany, in the American zone of Germany. And children from other camps came to visit and look at what we'd done. Well, this is me during election time. This was where I was told to go and look around the beautiful scenery while the people at the camp signed in for the rations they were going to get. And of course they got triple or double rations because I wasn't there to supervise that each person had two or three signatures. This is the first [NON-ENGLISH] I did that took place in Germany after the war. It was organized by the Jewish agency, with [NON-ENGLISH] on the extreme right, Misha in the middle, and [NON-ENGLISH]. And this is the UNRRA personnel and AJD personnel partaking in the Seder. Miss Richman is looking at Misha. Misha's on the right, and then there's Miss Richman and then myself. And this took place in 1947. [NON-ENGLISH] 1947. And this was the Nachum Levine, who was one of the big shots in the Jewish agency in Palestine that came to officiate at the service.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think this is [INAUDIBLE]. What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the protest that took place in '47 where the children-- children of all the world, help us get our-- out of camps. We want to be free as you are in our own country, which is rather strange, because it was in their country that the British were free. And in the background are the barracks where they lived. That was the building where they were housed in dormitories.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Rosenheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rosenheim, yes. In Rosenheim camp. And this is just a picture of their protest. They walked round and round the Rosenheim grounds, the camp grounds. And in the presence of journalists, and I think the congressmen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who took this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andy. Andy Sarason, our transport officer. The one that was so sympathetic to [NON-ENGLISH] requests. He took most of the-- the good pictures. I took the poor pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a holiday camp in Mittenwald. The first holiday camp that the children from Bad Reichenhall, not Rosenheim, were given. Rosenheim had, at this-- by this time, closed down. A lot of the children had gone on the Exodus ship that was hijacked, if you can call it. Taken by the British, and put-- the children were brought back to a British zone of Germany. And the staff of Rosenheim were transferred elsewhere. I was transferred to Bad Reichenhall, and we took the children-- well, some of the children of Bad Reichenhall, on their first holiday camp in Mittenwald. Mittenwald is near Garmisch And this was very, very good. It was the first time that they'd really been in the sun and had a proper holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in February 1947, just before I left for home. No, sorry. This must have been February '48, before I left for home.
